methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $65M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Peak Energy, a Series A climate and energy company founded by Landon Mossburg and Liam O'Connor, has raised $65M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a peer universe of three same-stage, same-sector private comparables (Graphyte, Fourth Power, and Steady Energy) plus broader Climate & Energy sector benchmarking, alongside one public comparable.

Two methods were applied to triangulate value. The stage-progression approach, using total capital raised as a proxy base, applies a 2.0x multiplier typical for Series A advancement, yielding $130M. The peer-median method examines three directly comparable Series A companies in Climate & Energy; their valuations range from $110M (Steady Energy) to $200M (Graphyte), with a median of $150M. These two methods—stage-progression at $130M and peer-median at $150M—converge tightly around the midpoint.

The triangulated valuation range is $110M (low) to $172M (high), with a median of $140M. The tightness of the two-method range reflects consistency between capital-progression assumptions and peer-market pricing at the Series A stage. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the three-company peer set given sector heterogeneity, the appropriate step-up multiplier for this specific subsector, and the extent to which recent peer valuations reflect current market conditions versus historical pricing. The $30M spread between low and high reflects these structural uncertainties.
